summary: Dan Shapiro announces he's taking a leave of absence from Google, where he'd spent two years after Google acquired his startup Sparkbuy. He praises Google's culture, food, people, and internal accountability, calling it a genuinely magical place to work. He's leaving temporarily to finish a book about startup CEOs for O'Reilly Publishing and to work on a mysterious non-software product. The post serves as both a love letter to Google and a teaser for his next entrepreneurial chapter.
key_points: Google acquired Shapiro's startup Sparkbuy in 2011, and he led a dual role as PM on Google Ads and CEO of Google Comparison Inc.
He strongly endorses Google's culture, arguing that anyone at a large tech company who hasn't at least applied to Google has a 'nonoptimal' career strategy.
Google's internal accountability culture means employees openly challenge executives at company all-hands meetings.
He's taking leave to finish a long-overdue book on startup CEOs and to pursue an unnamed non-software product — likely what became Glowforge.
money_quotes: And all the rumors are true: Google is truly a magical place to be.
Actually having healthy food available all the time, for free – both in the literal and cognitive load sense – is amazing.

For each person outside the organization pointing their finger and yelling at something Google's done, there is guaranteed to be at least one Googler, standing up in a company all-hands, quietly and respectfully giving the executive team hell about the same thing.
I'd humbly submit that if you're working at a BigCo and you haven't at least applied to Google to check it out, your career strategy is nonoptimal.
I also have another, non-software product that… well, more on that shortly when it's ready to share.
So the fantastic folks at Google have agreed to let me take a leave of absence for a few months to scratch some of these itches.
key_insight: Even when working at the best big company in tech, the entrepreneurial itch to build something new eventually wins out.
